claims.claim_text: GitLab's January 31, 2017 outage was caused by accidental removal of the primary PostgreSQL data directory, and recovery ultimately depended on a roughly six-hour-old staging snapshot.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | line 28-30
claims.rationale: GitLab's public postmortem documents the accidental deletion, failed recovery procedures, and use of a six-hour-old LVM snapshot.

claims.claim_text: GitLab had five backup methods configured and none of them worked.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | line 30
claims.anchor_text: The team immediately looked to their backup systems: five different backup methods were configured. None of them worked.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The team immediately turned to several backup and recovery procedures, but none of them provided a quick, usable recovery path.
claims.rationale: GitLab's postmortem describes multiple broken recovery paths, but the exact claim of five methods is not backed by the primary source.

claims.claim_text: GitLab lost six hours of data: 5,037 merge requests, comments, issues, and snippets.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | line 32
claims.anchor_text: GitLab lost six hours of data: 5,037 merge requests, comments, issues, and snippets.
claims.suggested_rewrite: GitLab lost several hours of database changes and affected thousands of projects, comments, and user accounts.
claims.rationale: The primary postmortem gives different impact figures and categories, so this precise number should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The GitLab outage became a case study in every disaster recovery talk for the next five years.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | line 32
claims.anchor_text: The incident became a case study in every disaster recovery talk for the next five years.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The incident became a memorable example of why backup and restore procedures must be tested.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced universal claim about conference talks rather than a verifiable incident fact.

claims.claim_text: RTO is the maximum acceptable delay between service interruption and restoration, while RPO is the maximum acceptable amount of time since the last recovery point.
claims.rationale: AWS Well-Architected defines both RTO and RPO in the same wording used for disaster recovery planning.

claims.claim_text: etcd is Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data.
claims.span_hint: section: etcd Backup and Restore | line 90
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes documentation explicitly states that etcd is Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data.

claims.claim_text: For managed Kubernetes where etcd is not directly managed, Velero is the standard tool for backing up and restoring Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes.
claims.span_hint: section: Velero: Kubernetes-Native Backup and Restore | line 211
claims.anchor_text: For managed Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS) where you don't manage etcd directly, Velero is the standard tool for backing up and restoring Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For managed Kubernetes platforms where you do not manage etcd directly, Velero can back up and restore Kubernetes resources and persistent volumes.
claims.rationale: The capability is documented, but the popularity claim 'the standard tool' is not directly supported by a primary source.

claims.claim_text: When namespace filtering is used, Velero does not include cluster-scoped resources by default unless `--include-cluster-resources=true` is set.
claims.span_hint: section: Velero / Common Mistakes | line 318, line 623
claims.rationale: Velero's own documentation directly states this default behavior, but velero.io is not on the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Velero defaults to resource-only backup.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Persistent volume protection depends on your snapshot or file-system-backup configuration
claims.rationale: Current Velero behavior depends on snapshot support and FSB settings, so 'resource-only by default' is too broad and misleading.

claims.claim_text: Amazon ECR private registries support cross-Region replication.
claims.rationale: The ECR user guide explicitly states that Amazon ECR supports cross-Region replication for private registries.

claims.claim_text: A retail company had a four-hour RTO target but took eleven hours in a DR test, with the detailed timing breakdown listed here.
claims.span_hint: section: The War Story: When RTO Doesn't Match Reality | line 72-80
claims.anchor_text: A retail company set their RTO at 4 hours for their Kubernetes platform. During their annual DR test, the actual recovery took 11 hours.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team can set an RTO that looks achievable on paper and then discover during testing that actual recovery takes much longer.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with precise numbers, so the specifics should be removed.

claims.claim_text: AWS recommends a TTL of 60 seconds or less for Route 53 failover records associated with health checks.
claims.rationale: The Route 53 failover-record documentation recommends a TTL of 60 seconds or less when the record is associated with a health check.

claims.claim_text: With TTL=60s, most clients fail over within 90 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **With TTL=60s:** Most clients failover within 90 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With a lower TTL, clients usually pick up the DR endpoint faster after their cached record expires.
claims.rationale: Exact failover timing depends on resolver and client caching behavior, so this number is too precise to assert generally.

claims.claim_text: With TTL=300s, some clients can be stuck for up to 330 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **With TTL=300s:** Some clients stuck for up to 330 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With a higher TTL, clients can keep using cached records for several minutes after failover.
claims.rationale: The exact 330-second outcome is a simplified arithmetic estimate, not a generally guaranteed client behavior.

claims.claim_text: TTL=30s is the practical minimum because many resolvers ignore lower TTLs and cache for at least 30 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: TTL=30s is the practical minimum—below that, many resolvers ignore the TTL and cache for at least 30 seconds anyway.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Very low TTLs can still be limited by resolver caching behavior, so test failover behavior with the resolvers your users actually use.
claims.rationale: This makes a precise resolver-behavior claim without an authoritative source that directly supports it.

claims.claim_text: Velero was originally called Heptio Ark.
claims.rationale: The official GitHub repository README states that Velero was formerly Heptio Ark.

claims.claim_text: When VMware acquired Heptio in 2018, the project was renamed to Velero and donated to the CNCF.
claims.anchor_text: When VMware acquired Heptio in 2018, the project was renamed to Velero (Latin for "sail") and donated to the CNCF.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When VMware acquired Heptio, the project was renamed to Velero.
claims.rationale: The rename is supported, but the sentence incorrectly ties CNCF donation to the 2018 acquisition.

claims.claim_text: Velero is the de facto standard for Kubernetes backup, with over 8,000 GitHub stars and production use at thousands of organizations.
claims.anchor_text: It is now the de facto standard for Kubernetes backup, with over 8,000 GitHub stars and production use at thousands of organizations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It continued as an open source Kubernetes backup and restore project.
claims.rationale: The sentence mixes an unsupported superlative with uncited adoption claims.

claims.claim_text: etcd can restore a typical 10,000-15,000-object cluster state in under 60 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **etcd can handle a cluster state restore in under 60 seconds** for a typical cluster with 10,000-15,000 objects.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Restoring etcd data files is often faster than the reconciliation work that follows, so end-to-end cluster recovery usually takes longer than the raw restore step.
claims.rationale: This exact performance claim is not backed by an authoritative upstream benchmark.

claims.claim_text: AWS S3 Cross-Region Replication has a 99.99% SLA for replication within 15 minutes, and most objects replicate in under 30 seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **AWS S3 Cross-Region Replication has a 99.99% SLA for replication within 15 minutes,** but the actual replication latency for most objects is under 30 seconds.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Amazon S3 replication timing depends on the replication mode you choose, so verify it against your actual RPO requirements instead of assuming a short SLA-backed window.
claims.rationale: This conflates standard CRR with S3 Replication Time Control and overstates the SLA details.

claims.claim_text: S3 Replication Time Control guarantees that 99.99% of objects are replicated within 15 minutes.
claims.anchor_text: For critical RPO requirements, enable S3 Replication Time Control (RTC), which guarantees 99.99% of objects are replicated within 15 minutes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For stricter replication objectives, evaluate S3 Replication Time Control rather than relying on default cross-region replication behavior.
claims.rationale: The original sentence overstates RTC as a guarantee and uses the wrong precision for the SLA-backed commitment.

claims.claim_text: GitLab publicly streamed part of the 2017 recovery effort on YouTube.
claims.rationale: GitLab's postmortem states that the team streamed the recovery procedure on YouTube.

claims.claim_text: The video became one of the most-watched incident response recordings in tech history and inspired hundreds of companies to test their backups.
claims.anchor_text: The video became one of the most-watched incident response recordings in tech history and directly inspired hundreds of companies to test their backup procedures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The incident reinforced the importance of testing backup procedures before a real outage.
claims.rationale: These impact claims are not directly sourceable from the primary incident record.

claims.claim_text: GitLab's later postmortem became a template for incident documentation.
claims.anchor_text: GitLab later published a detailed post-mortem that became a template for incident documentation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: GitLab later documented the outage in a detailed public postmortem.
claims.rationale: The existence of the postmortem is sourceable, but the 'template for incident documentation' claim is not.

further_reading.title: GitLab 2017 Database Outage Postmortem
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary incident record for the backup failures, live-streamed recovery, and operational lessons used in this module.

further_reading.title: Operating etcd Clusters for Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Upstream reference for etcd's role in Kubernetes and the supported backup and restore workflow.

further_reading.title: Disaster Recovery Options in the Cloud
further_reading.why_relevant: Maps backup-and-restore, pilot light, warm standby, and active-active patterns to RTO/RPO trade-offs.

further_reading.title: Route 53 Failover Record Values
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for failover-record TTL guidance in the DNS section.

notes: No shared citation pool exists for this section in the repo. Supported claims use proposed_url directly; Velero resource-filtering behavior requires allowlist expansion because the primary source is velero.io. Diagrams, code blocks, quiz answers, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded.
